blocks.content: By the time they got on their bikes and started back toward the residential streets, the storm's leading edge was moving faster than it had any right to move and the first fat drops of rain had started hitting the pavement — scattered, slow, one every few feet, like the sky was deciding before it committed.

It came down the way Southern summer storms do — not gradually but all at once, as if someone opened a valve. The light went from amber to gray to a bruised violet in about thirty seconds. The oak trees along the street bent sideways. The gutters, dry for three weeks, took about ninety seconds to fill and then run, red clay water moving along the curb in a fast shallow current.
They were soaked before they'd made it one block.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: It wasn't supposed to come this fast!

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You were not right last time!

Eli was whooping ahead of them, which was his response to weather events. He'd decided somewhere around age eight that being alarmed by things was less interesting than being enthusiastic about them, and had never revised this opinion. Savannah had pulled her shirt up over her head, which accomplished very little except giving her somewhere to direct her dignity.

They ducked under the overhang of the hardware store on Cedar Street, all four bikes clattering against the wall, everyone breathing hard from the sprint. The rain hammered the street in front of them — the sound of it on the metal awning was almost too loud to talk over.
Eli shook his head like a wet dog. Water went in every direction.
They stood there, dripping, watching the street disappear behind a gray curtain of rain.
Daisy was already looking at Kit.
